title: Collapse during a startling alarm
diagnosis: Long QT syndrome with torsades de pointes

chart.cc: Passed out and woke up on the floor
chart.oneLiner: 24F presents after a brief loss of consciousness triggered by a sudden loud alarm, with a family history of unexplained sudden death and recent palpitations.

opening: A fire alarm went off, my heart started racing, and the next thing I knew I was waking up on the floor.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 24-year-old woman, now alert and back to baseline, anxious. Good historian.

HPI: Brief loss of consciousness immediately after a sudden LOUD fire alarm; felt a few seconds of palpitations/fluttering before going down. Witnessed brief generalized shaking for a few seconds, but recovered QUICKLY with NO post-event confusion, NO tongue biting, NO incontinence (favors convulsive syncope over a primary seizure). Two prior near-fainting spells — one while startled by a phone ringing, one during a swim. NO chest pain, NO true aura, NO focal weakness.
FAMILY HISTORY (only if asked about family/sudden death): a brother died suddenly in his sleep at age 19 (unexplained); a maternal cousin 'drowned' while swimming; no known deafness in the family.
PMH: occasional palpitations; otherwise healthy. MEDS: recently started an antibiotic for a sinus infection and takes an over-the-counter antihistamine; uses an antiemetic occasionally (only if asked about medications — these can prolong the QT). NO recreational drugs. SOCIAL: social alcohol only, non-smoker.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: alert, anxious-appearing, no distress; no dysmorphic features.
- Vitals: BP 112/70, HR 82 and regular, RR 16, 36.8°C, SpO2 99% RA.
- Cardiac: regular rate and rhythm, normal S1/S2, NO murmur, NO gallop; peripheral pulses normal and symmetric.
- Neuro: fully oriented, no focal deficit, no tongue laceration, normal gait.
- Hearing: normal to conversation (no congenital deafness).

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the family history of sudden death/drowning unless asked about family history or sudden death in relatives.
- Do NOT volunteer the new antibiotic/antihistamine/antiemetic unless asked about medications or recent new drugs.
- Emphasize (only if asked about triggers/warning) that it happened right after a sudden loud noise and was preceded by palpitations.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.EKG_QTc: 12-lead QTc markedly prolonged at 530 ms (normal <460 ms in women); broad-based T waves. Diagnostic of QT prolongation.
labs.Telemetry: Captured a self-terminating run of polymorphic VT with a twisting/undulating QRS axis around the baseline (torsades de pointes), preceded by a pause.
labs.BMP_Mg: K 3.3 (LOW), Mg 1.4 mg/dL (LOW), Ca 8.4 (low-normal), Cr normal — electrolyte derangements that potentiate QT prolongation.
labs.Troponin: Negative — no acute ischemic pattern to explain the arrhythmia.
labs.Tox_screen: Urine drug screen negative — no stimulant/cocaine effect.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Markedly prolonged QT interval (QTc ~530 ms) with broad-based T waves in a patient in sinus rhythm; substrate for torsades de pointes.
ekg.verify: From the LITFL 'QT interval / Long QT syndrome' library page — download an example tracing that matches: markedly prolonged QT interval (QTc ~530 ms) with broad T waves in sinus rhythm. Self-host with credit to litfl.com; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.
ekg.imageDescription: 12-lead EKG showing a markedly prolonged QT interval with broad T waves (long QT syndrome)

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Normal cardiac silhouette and clear lung fields; no cardiomegaly, effusion, or acute process (a normal film is itself a meaningful read in primary electrical disease).
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: normal PA chest radiograph with a normal cardiac silhouette and clear lungs. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential for this syncope (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Cardiac syncope due to a ventricular arrhythmia from a prolonged QT / torsades (leading & CANT-MISS,9). Other inherited arrhythmia / structural cardiac cause — HCM, Brugada, catecholaminergic VT (CANT-MISS,4). Seizure / epilepsy (CANT-MISS,3). Vasovagal syncope (core,2). Drug- or electrolyte-induced QT prolongation as a mechanism (core,2). Recognizes that syncope triggered by a startle/exertion preceded by palpitations with a family history of sudden death points to a primary electrical (channelopathy) cause (core,5).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Cardiac syncope from a ventricular arrhythmia due to a prolonged QT interval (torsades de pointes). Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Other inherited arrhythmia/structural disease (HCM, Brugada, CPVT); Seizure/epilepsy. Also reasonable: Vasovagal syncope; Drug- or electrolyte-induced QT prolongation.

criticalActions.item: Cardiac syncope from ventricular arrhythmia due to prolonged QT / torsades de pointes
Other inherited arrhythmia / structural cardiac disease (HCM, Brugada, CPVT)

coreActions.item: Drug- or electrolyte-induced QT prolongation as a mechanism
Recognizes that startle/exertional syncope preceded by palpitations with a family history of sudden death points to a primary electrical (channelopathy) cause

mustNotMiss: Other inherited arrhythmia / structural cardiac disease (HCM, Brugada, CPVT)
acceptableAlternatives: Drug- or electrolyte-induced QT prolongation as a mechanism

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: 12-lead EKG immediately with measured/corrected QT (CANT-MISS — diagnostic,6). Continuous telemetry / cardiac monitoring to capture arrhythmia (CANT-MISS,4). BMP with magnesium and calcium (electrolytes that prolong QT) (2). Review/stop QT-prolonging medications (2). TSH and a urine tox screen (1). PENALTY: discharging or failing to monitor a patient with syncope and palpitations (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: 12-lead EKG immediately with a measured QTc; Continuous telemetry / cardiac monitoring; BMP with magnesium and calcium; review and stop QT-prolonging drugs.

penalties.item: discharging or failing to monitor a patient with syncope and palpitations

contraindicatedTests: discharging or failing to monitor a patient with syncope and palpitations

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings (be specific about the intervals).
steps.rubric: Findings = markedly prolonged QT interval (QTc ~530 ms) in sinus rhythm — the substrate for torsades. CREDIT: measures/identifies the QT and recognizes it is prolonged (6); corrects for rate (QTc) rather than raw QT (4); notes broad T-wave morphology / sinus rhythm (3); systematic read (2). PENALTY: calling the tracing normal or missing the long QT (-3).
keyFindings: notes broad T waves in sinus rhythm
criticalFindings: Markedly prolonged QT interval (QTc ~530 ms) in sinus rhythm — the substrate for torsades de pointes.
commonMistakes: reading the raw QT without correcting for heart rate (QTc)
steps.idealAnswer: Sinus rhythm with a markedly prolonged QT interval (QTc ~530 ms) and broad T waves — long QT, the substrate for torsades de pointes.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: markedly prolonged QT interval (QTc ~530 ms) in sinus rhythm — the substrate for torsades de pointes.

coreActions.item: notes broad T waves in sinus rhythm

penalties.item: calling the tracing normal or missing the long QT

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = normal film. CREDIT: identifies a normal/near-normal film (6); notes the normal heart size helps exclude structural disease/cardiomyopathy as a cause (4). PENALTY: inventing pathology (-2).
keyFindings: notes normal heart size helps exclude structural cardiac disease
criticalFindings: Normal cardiac silhouette and clear lungs — no acute cardiopulmonary process.
commonMistakes: inventing cardiomegaly or an effusion on a normal film
steps.idealAnswer: Normal chest radiograph with a normal cardiac silhouette and clear lungs; supports a primary electrical (channelopathy) cause rather than structural disease.

criticalActions: Normal cardiac silhouette and clear lungs — no acute cardiopulmonary process.
coreActions: notes normal heart size helps exclude structural cardiac disease

penalties.label: invents pathology on a normal film

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: QTc 530 ms, telemetry capturing a self-terminating run of polymorphic VT with a twisting axis, low potassium and magnesium, and a family history of sudden death. What is your diagnosis and why is it dangerous?
steps.rubric: Long QT syndrome with torsades de pointes (6). Explains the danger — torsades can degenerate into ventricular fibrillation and cause sudden cardiac death; QT-prolonging drugs and low K/Mg potentiate it (4).
steps.idealAnswer: Long QT syndrome with torsades de pointes. Dangerous because torsades can degenerate into ventricular fibrillation and cause sudden cardiac death; it is potentiated by QT-prolonging drugs and by hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia, which must be corrected and avoided.
mustNotMiss: Degeneration of torsades into ventricular fibrillation / sudden cardiac death
acceptableAlternatives: Congenital long QT syndrome unmasked by QT-prolonging drugs and hypokalemia/hypomagnesemia

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH torsades / long QT: IV magnesium sulfate for torsades regardless of serum level (CANT-MISS,7); stop ALL QT-prolonging drugs (CANT-MISS,5); aggressively replete potassium and magnesium (4); continuous telemetry on a monitored bed (3); increase heart rate to shorten QT — isoproterenol or overdrive/temporary pacing for recurrent pause-dependent torsades (3); cardiology/EP consult for beta-blocker and ICD evaluation in congenital LQTS (2); unsynchronized defibrillation if torsades becomes sustained/unstable (1). PENALTY: giving a QT-prolonging antiarrhythmic such as amiodarone/sotalol/procainamide (-3); discharging the patient (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Give IV magnesium sulfate (first-line for torsades, even if the magnesium level is normal). Immediately stop all QT-prolonging drugs and aggressively replete potassium and magnesium. Keep on continuous telemetry in a monitored bed. For recurrent pause-dependent torsades, increase the heart rate with isoproterenol or temporary overdrive pacing. If torsades becomes sustained/hemodynamically unstable, perform unsynchronized defibrillation. Cardiology/EP consult: beta-blocker and consideration of an ICD for congenital long QT syndrome, plus family screening. Avoid all QT-prolonging agents (e.g., amiodarone, sotalol).

coreActions.item: Continuous telemetry on a monitored bed
Increase heart rate (isoproterenol / overdrive pacing) for pause-dependent torsades
Cardiology/EP consult for beta-blocker and ICD evaluation

bonusActions.item: Unsynchronized defibrillation if torsades becomes sustained/unstable

optionalActions: Continuous telemetry on a monitored bed
Increase heart rate (isoproterenol / overdrive pacing) for pause-dependent torsades
Cardiology/EP consult for beta-blocker and ICD evaluation
Unsynchronized defibrillation if torsades becomes sustained/unstable

standardizedPatient.communicationStyle: answers directly but does not volunteer extra information
standardizedPatient.affect: 24-year-old woman, now alert and back to baseline, anxious. Good historian.
disclosureRules: Do not reveal sensitive history unless directly asked
Do not mention or hint at the diagnosis
Do not volunteer hidden exam or diagnostic findings
Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing

historyTriggers.response: My brother died suddenly in his sleep when he was 19 — they never found out why. And a cousin on my mum's side drowned while swimming.
I just started an antibiotic for a sinus infection a few days ago, and I take an over-the-counter allergy pill. I sometimes use an anti-nausea tablet too.
A fire alarm went off right next to me. I felt my heart fluttering for a couple of seconds, then everything went black.
They said I twitched for a few seconds, but I came right back to normal — no confusion, I didn't bite my tongue and I didn't wet myself.
Twice before I nearly fainted — once when a phone rang and startled me, and once while I was swimming.
Yes — I get little flutters in my chest now and then, and right before I went down my heart was racing.
No chest pain and no trouble breathing.
No, my hearing is fine — no one in my family is deaf either.
No, I don't use any recreational or street drugs.

caseSummary.finalDiagnosis: Long QT syndrome with torsades de pointes
caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 24F presents after a brief loss of consciousness triggered by a sudden loud alarm, with a family history of unexplained sudden death and recent palpitations. The diagnosis is Long QT syndrome with torsades de pointes, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyHistoryClues: Syncope triggered by a sudden loud noise (startle), preceded by palpitations.
Prior near-faints with auditory startle and during swimming (LQT2/LQT1 triggers).
Family history of unexplained sudden death and a relative who drowned while swimming.
Recently started a QT-prolonging antibiotic plus OTC antihistamine/antiemetic.
Brief convulsive movements but rapid recovery without post-ictal confusion, tongue biting, or incontinence.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Cardiac: regular rate and rhythm, normal S1/S2, NO murmur, NO gallop.
Neuro: fully oriented, no focal deficit, no tongue laceration, normal gait.
Cardiac: peripheral pulses normal and symmetric.
General: alert, anxious-appearing, no distress; no dysmorphic features.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items (family sudden death, new QT-prolonging drugs) only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Long QT syndrome with torsades de pointes while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Long QT syndrome with torsades de pointes and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.
commonPitfalls: Mislabeling convulsive syncope as a primary seizure and discharging without telemetry
Reading the raw QT without correcting for heart rate (QTc)
managementPearls: Replete potassium and magnesium; increase heart rate for pause-dependent torsades
criticalInterpretationItems: Markedly prolonged QT interval (QTc ~530 ms) in sinus rhythm — the substrate for torsades de pointes.
Normal cardiac silhouette and clear lungs — no acute cardiopulmonary process.
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Markedly prolonged QT interval (QTc ~530 ms) in sinus rhythm — the substrate for torsades de pointes.
Normal cardiac silhouette and clear lungs — no acute cardiopulmonary process.
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Markedly prolonged QT interval (QTc ~530 ms) in sinus rhythm — the substrate for torsades de pointes.
Normal cardiac silhouette and clear lungs — no acute cardiopulmonary process.
imageInterpretationPearls: A normal film is an interpretation task in its own right — a normal heart size supports a primary electrical cause over structural disease.
Always correct the QT for heart rate (QTc) before calling it normal or prolonged.
Read every study systematically and state pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
